content_en:
The zakah chapters of Al-Majmu' represent some of the most practically important material in the work, as zakah — the obligatory annual alms — affects every Muslim of sufficient wealth. Al-Nawawi's treatment is comprehensive, covering the types of property subject to zakah, the thresholds (nisab) at which zakah becomes obligatory, the rates of payment, and the eight categories of recipients specified in the Quran.

Property Subject to zakah

Al-Nawawi identifies the main categories of property on which zakah is obligatory: (1) gold and silver (and by extension, currency); (2) trade goods (urud al-tijarah); (3) livestock — camels, cattle, and sheep/goats; (4) agricultural produce — grains and fruits; and (5) treasures found in the earth (rikaz). For each category, he specifies the conditions that make zakah obligatory: the property must reach the minimum threshold (nisab), must be owned outright and freely disposable, and must have been in the owner's possession for a full lunar year (hawl) — except for agricultural produce, where zakah is due at harvest regardless of the time of ownership.

The threshold for gold is twenty mithqals (approximately 85 grams by modern calculation), and for silver 200 dirhams (approximately 595 grams). For trade goods, the nisab is assessed by reference to the value of either gold or silver. The rates of zakah vary: 2.5% on gold, silver, and trade goods; a graduated scale on livestock depending on number and type; and 10% on rain-watered crops (or 5% on irrigated crops, reflecting the greater effort involved).

The Eight Categories of Recipients

Al-Nawawi's treatment of zakah distribution follows the Quranic verse (9:60) that specifies eight categories of recipients. He discusses each category in detail: (1) the poor (fuqara'), those who lack sufficient means; (2) the destitute (masakin), those who lack any means at all; (3) those employed to collect zakah (amilun alayha); (4) those whose hearts are to be reconciled (mu'allafat qulubuhum); (5) to free slaves (fi al-riqab); (6) those in debt (al-gharimun); (7) in the cause of God (fi sabil Allah); and (8) the wayfarer (ibn al-sabil).

A central debate in the Shafi'i school is whether zakah must be distributed among all eight categories or may be concentrated on one or a few. Al-Nawawi presents the position of the majority of Shafi'i scholars that when one is distributing local zakah oneself (rather than through a central authority), one must distribute to representatives of all present categories, and that it is not permissible to give all the zakah to a single person from a single category.

zakah al-Fitr

Al-Nawawi also covers zakah al-fitr, the obligatory charity given at the end of Ramadan. Unlike regular zakah, zakah al-fitr is a fixed amount per person — one sa' (approximately 3 kg) of the staple food of the region per Muslim under one's financial responsibility. It must be given before the Eid prayer on the day of Eid al-Fitr. Al-Nawawi discusses whether it may be given in cash (which the Shafi'i school generally does not allow, unlike the Hanafi school) and the rationale for requiring payment in food: it purifies the fasting person and provides direct sustenance to the poor at the time of celebration.
content_id:
Bab-bab zakat dalam Al-Majmu mewakili beberapa materi yang paling penting secara praktis dalam karya ini, karena zakat — sedekah tahunan yang diwajibkan — memengaruhi setiap Muslim yang memiliki kekayaan yang cukup. Perlakuan al-Nawawi bersifat komprehensif, mencakup jenis-jenis harta yang dikenai zakat, ambang batas (nisab) di mana zakat menjadi wajib, tarif pembayaran, dan delapan golongan penerima yang ditentukan dalam Al-Quran.

Harta yang Dikenai Zakat

Al-Nawawi mengidentifikasi kategori-kategori utama harta yang wajib dizakati: (1) emas dan perak (dan dengan perluasannya, mata uang); (2) barang-barang dagangan (urud al-tijarah); (3) ternak — unta, sapi, dan kambing/domba; (4) hasil pertanian — biji-bijian dan buah-buahan; dan (5) harta karun yang ditemukan di dalam tanah (rikaz). Untuk setiap kategori, beliau menetapkan syarat-syarat yang mewajibkan zakat: harta harus mencapai ambang batas minimum (nisab), harus dimiliki sepenuhnya dan dapat digunakan secara bebas, dan harus telah berada dalam kepemilikan pemiliknya selama satu tahun lunar penuh (haul) — kecuali untuk hasil pertanian, di mana zakat jatuh tempo saat panen terlepas dari masa kepemilikan.

Ambang batas untuk emas adalah dua puluh mitsqal (sekitar 85 gram menurut perhitungan modern), dan untuk perak 200 dirham (sekitar 595 gram). Untuk barang-barang dagangan, nisab dinilai berdasarkan nilai emas atau perak. Tarif zakat bervariasi: 2,5% untuk emas, perak, dan barang-barang dagangan; skala bertingkat untuk ternak tergantung pada jumlah dan jenisnya; dan 10% untuk tanaman yang diairi hujan (atau 5% untuk tanaman yang diairi irigasi, mencerminkan upaya yang lebih besar).

Delapan Golongan Penerima

Perlakuan al-Nawawi tentang distribusi zakat mengikuti ayat Al-Quran (9:60) yang menetapkan delapan golongan penerima. Beliau mendiskusikan setiap golongan secara terperinci: (1) orang-orang fakir, mereka yang kekurangan sarana yang cukup; (2) orang-orang miskin, mereka yang sama sekali tidak memiliki sarana; (3) mereka yang dipekerjakan untuk mengumpulkan zakat (amilun alayha); (4) mereka yang hatinya ingin didamaikan (muallafat qulubuhum); (5) untuk membebaskan budak (fi al-riqab); (6) mereka yang berhutang (al-gharimun); (7) di jalan Allah (fi sabilillah); dan (8) orang dalam perjalanan (ibnu sabil).

Perdebatan sentral dalam mazhab Syafi'i adalah apakah zakat harus didistribusikan di antara semua delapan golongan atau boleh dipusatkan pada satu atau beberapa. Al-Nawawi menyajikan posisi mayoritas ulama Syafi'i bahwa ketika seseorang mendistribusikan zakat lokal sendiri (bukan melalui otoritas pusat), ia harus mendistribusikan kepada perwakilan semua golongan yang ada, dan tidak diperbolehkan memberikan semua zakat kepada satu orang dari satu golongan.

Zakat al-Fithr

Al-Nawawi juga mencakup zakat al-fithr, sedekah wajib yang diberikan di akhir Ramadan. Tidak seperti zakat biasa, zakat al-fithr adalah jumlah tetap per orang — satu sha (sekitar 3 kg) dari makanan pokok daerah per Muslim yang berada di bawah tanggung jawab finansial seseorang. Ia harus diberikan sebelum shalat Ied pada hari Ied al-Fithr. Al-Nawawi mendiskusikan apakah boleh diberikan dalam bentuk uang tunai (yang umumnya tidak diizinkan oleh mazhab Syafi'i, berbeda dengan mazhab Hanafi) dan alasan untuk mewajibkan pembayaran dalam bentuk makanan: ia menyucikan orang yang berpuasa dan memberikan nafkah langsung kepada orang miskin pada saat perayaan.
content_ar: The الزكاة الأبواب of Al-Majmu' represent some of the most practically المهم material in the work, as الزكاة — the obligatory annual الصدقة — affects every مسلم of sufficient الثروة. النووي's treatment is comprehensive, covering the types of property subject to الزكاة, the thresholds ( nisab ) at which الزكاة becomes obligatory, the rates of payment, and the eight categories of recipients specified in the القرآن الكريم. Property Subject to الزكاة النووي identifies the الرئيسي categories of property on which الزكاة is obligatory: (1) gold and silver (and by extension, currency); (2) trade goods ( urud al-tijarah ); (3) livestock — camels, cattle, and sheep/goats; (4) agricultural produce — grains and fruits; and (5) treasures found in the earth ( rikaz ). For each category, he specifies the conditions that make الزكاة obligatory: the property must reach the minimum threshold ( nisab ), must be owned outright and freely disposable, and must have been in the owner's possession for a full lunar year ( hawl ) — except for agricultural produce, where الزكاة is due at harvest regardless of the time of ownership. The threshold for gold is twenty mithqals (approximately 85 grams by modern calculation), and for silver 200 dirhams (approximately 595 grams). For trade goods, the nisab is assessed by reference to the value of either gold or silver. The rates of الزكاة vary: 2.5% on gold, silver, and trade goods; a graduated scale on livestock depending on number and type; and 10% on rain-watered crops (or 5% on irrigated crops, reflecting the greater effort involved). The Eight Categories of Recipients النووي's treatment of الزكاة distribution follows the القرآني الآية (9:60) that specifies eight categories of recipients. He discusses each category in detail: (1) the poor ( fuqara' ), those who lack sufficient means; (2) the destitute ( masakin ), those who lack any means at all; (3) those employed to collect الزكاة ( amilun alayha ); (4) those whose القلوب are to be reconciled ( mu'allafat qulubuhum ); (5) to free slaves ( fi al-riqab ); (6) those in debt ( al-gharimun ); (7) in the cause of God ( fi sabil الله ); and (8) the wayfarer ( ibn al-sabil ). A المحوري debate in the الشافعي school is whether الزكاة must be distributed among all eight categories or may be concentrated on one or a few. النووي presents the position of the majority of الشافعي العلماء that عندما one is distributing local الزكاة oneself (rather than through a المحوري authority), على المرء distribute to representatives of all present categories, and that لا يجوز to give all the الزكاة to a single person from a single category. الزكاة al-Fitr النووي أيضاً covers الزكاة al-fitr, the obligatory الصدقة given at the end of Ramadan. Unlike regular الزكاة, الزكاة al-fitr is a fixed amount per person —
